title: Elasticity
summary: 9609 A Level — PED, YED, XED calculations and strategic pricing implications.
learningObjectives: Explain revenue effects of price changes.

simpleExplanation.title: How sensitive demand is
simpleExplanation.summary: Elasticity measures responsiveness of demand to price (PED), income (YED), or other products' prices (XED). It guides pricing, product mix, and competitive strategy.
steps: PED = % change in Qd ÷ % change in price.
YED: normal goods positive; inferior goods negative.
XED positive for substitutes; negative for complements.

flashcards.back: |PED| > 1 — quantity changes more than proportionately to price.
Total revenue falls — lost volume outweighs higher price.
Total revenue rises — volume drop is small.
Normal good — demand rises as income rises.
Substitutes — rival's price rise boosts your demand.
Substitutes, necessity, % of income, time period.

sections.content: **Elasticity** (8.1.1) opens **Topic 8**. Always **show calculation** then **apply to pricing decision** — link to **3.1.2 demand** and **3.3.3 price**.

**PED** = % Δ Qd ÷ % Δ P

**YED** = % Δ Qd ÷ % Δ income

**XED** = % Δ Qd of good A ÷ % Δ P of good B

sections.question: Price rises from $10 to $12 (+20%). Quantity falls from 1,000 to 850 units (−15%). Calculate PED and advise on revenue.
sections.solution:
**PED** = −15% ÷ +20% = **−0.75** (inelastic, |PED| < 1)

**Revenue before:** 10 × 1,000 = **$10,000**
**Revenue after:** 12 × 850 = **$10,200**

**Advice:** Demand **inelastic** — **price increase raises revenue** short term. **Caution:** Longer term competitors may attract customers; **brand loyalty** may weaken — monitor **market share** (3.1.6).

sections.content: State **sign** of PED (usually negative) but compare **absolute value** to 1 for elastic/inelastic.
